definition: The 1970s is the most relevant historical analogue for current commodity dynamics (7/10 similarity score) sharing capex drought, cartel dynamics, monetary regime stress, geopolitical supply shocks, and above-target inflation. Key differences: no energy transition demand headwind for oil existed then, labor unions amplified wage-price spirals in ways absent now, and commodity intensity of GDP has declined ~40% since, muting inflation transmission.
evidence: Shared features: capex drought, cartel dynamics, monetary regime stress, geopolitical shocks
Commodity intensity of GDP down ~40% since 1970s
Absence of energy transition demand headwind in 1970s
Labor union amplification absent in current cycle
Pair_0 confirmed useful for quantitative anchors but not mechanical application
open_questions: Which analogue is playing out? Falsifiable: copper >$12k + oil >$100 = 1970s; copper <$10k + oil <$85 = muted cycle
Do quantitative base rates from prior cycles (200-400% real returns, 13-22 year duration) apply given structural differences?
